session for Mike Harding show. Recorded: 1971-05-31. First TX: 1971-06-08.
session for Bob Harris show. Recorded 1972-02-23. First TX: 1972-03-06. Both tracks available on The BBC Sessions (BBC Worldwide). [http://rateyourmusic.com/release/album/argent/the_bbc_sessions/]

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. This is TV on the Radio, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, the first ever Radio 1 session from the Birmingham-based band called Cryer, and some archive sessions by a bunch of musicians who still contribute enormously to the music scene.
Collectively, years ago, they were known as Argent.
We've got some new releases by The Penetrations, by Dead Ringer, by Camel, Crocus, and Herman Brew.
Classic cuts by Whitesnake, Thin Lizzy, REO Speedwagon, Deke Leonard, Foreigner, among others.
Not forgetting our Friday Night Connection.
Tonight it's a long one, and we'll titillate, but we start with this.
Whitesnake, as if you didn't know.
From the album Love Hunter, that was called Medicine Man.
I played that specially for loads of people, Claire Vernon and Claire Barron,
and all the Royston Rockers in Hertfordshire.
also for Martin Brooks who lives in High Wycombe in Bucks and all his mates
Guy Cornelius who lives in Pinner wants me to say hello to Jem and Roger and Mark and Don
and everybody at the Merchant Taylor School
which I've just done and I'm happy to do so
across the water in Dungannon, County Tyrone in Northern Ireland
hello, good evening to Patrick Marsh, Timothy Paisley, Brian Turner, Andrew McNamee and Stooge
still across the water but this time in Craighaven in County Armagh in Ireland
Hello to Terrence Gray, the fiancé of Yvonne Robertson,
who also demands that I mention everybody at the Lismore Comprehensive School.
Still across the water, in Ballymoney in Northern Ireland,
Whitesnake's well-popular over there mentions four.
All the people in Coleraine who went to the Wild Horses gig,
including Bino and Jacko and Bert and Sammy.
Sammy now. Hello there, Sammy.
And Andrew and Jonathan and Alastair and Alan and David.
And the gentleman who wrote the letter by the name of David Padine.
The track you heard before that was from a band who are currently on tour in this country.
Going to see them Tuesday at the Hammersmith Odeon.
They're on tour with Diamondhead, another tasty band.
The band you heard were April Wine, a track from their new album,
which is called The Nature of the Beast.
It was called Crash and Burn.
It's 11 minutes after 10 on the Friday Rock Show.
Here's the first of four by Cryer.
The title track from the album Bad Reputation,
played because Nick Morphine and Alan Pollyfella,
an Anthony Radish rapper
who had bangers in and around Maidstone
wrote me a little card and they said
no play, no more listen to
shock. Can't have that.
And before that you heard something called
The Visionary by Cryer from our session
by them. They're a band who are based in Birmingham
to the best of my recollection that track was actually
named by a member of the
public and I think I'm right
in recollecting that the member of the public
who came up with the title called The Visionary
actually got himself a
free flying V guitar.
I hope I'm right.
Here's some archive material recorded by Argent in 1971 and 1972.
Lengthy studio version of Hold Your Head Up.
It was a hit for that band way back in 1972.
That was Argent out of the archives, recorded in 1972.
And before that, you heard Argent again on a track called Yerenes.
That was recorded in 1971.
Argent had been requested by Al Spring-Curry, Mark the Bolt, Dave Conk,
Mick Gaynor, Chick Formby, John Whip, and Guy the Ugly Duck.
And all the other people who hate going to the school,
it seems is run by a gentleman called Mr. Monkey Hastings, I think it is,
of Lion House.
The school is the King's School in Bruton.
All I've got to tell you, fellas, is everybody has to do it,
so you might as well enjoy it.
This coming Sunday, I believe I'm right in saying, is Burns Night.
Boy, would I like to be in Scotland.
As this coming Sunday is Burns Night,
we thought a scots band ought to be played and here's a new one called the penetrations
on 1970 to 1980 a track entitled lightning demanded by paul gribble who lives at and i
hope i can say this right trevathian 76 mile house road plymouth down there in beautiful devon
the barns also when i say hello to everybody who attends the fifth form at devonport high school
Must be a stool. Must be. Good track.
And before that you heard a single that we only got through the post the other day.
It's put together by Kick Productions of Kilmarnock in Scotland.
According to their press handout, the one they sent me anyway, it's the Sassenach version.
And I love this bit that they write.
From Kilmarnock and Ayrshire come the Penetrations,
who are best not described by Scotland's National Bard as
we sleek it cowering timorous beasties for they are anything but.
George Wilson on the vocals, Paul McCluskey on the guitar, Ewan Wallace on the guitar, Stuart Dunlop on the bass and Stephen Cusack on the drums.
The Penetration's single is out on kick and it was called Coming to You and it's very good.
In our humble opinion, so are Cryer.
The Iceberg. First came out in the early 70s. It's just been revitalized and reissued on the United Artists label in their Rockfile series.
And the track we played you there from the album called Iceberg was Razorblade.
Gorsiek Leonard was one of the original members of Man, a really great Welsh band.
He was in the band from 1968 to 1972.
And here's the cryptic message of the night.
Hi, Errol. How's the course?
And before that, something from our session by Cryer.
The track we played was called Spaces.
Seven and a half minutes to eleven. It's Friday Night Connection time.
Right, the tracks we played you last week were these.
Hammerhead, from the album Head On by Samson.
I'm Mandy. Fly me, from How Dare You by 10cc.
and Great White Hope from Pieces of Eight by Styx.
The winner is, who gets the £10 record token, of course, Paul Hardy.
Outnumber 8, Shafto Close, Delves Lane, Concert and County Durham.
Well done, Paul.
The answer was, of course, Hammerhead, Great White Hope
and in the lyrics of I, Mandy, Fly Me, 10cc mentioned a shark.
Answer, sharks.
Loads of replies this week.
Thank you very much for writing in.
I really do mean that because it costs money to send a letter in this day and age.

Only one winner, though, and the winner is Paul Hardy, as I said, of Concert in County Durham, and he's put together his own Friday Night Connection as well. It's a long one, a good one musically, and inside it somewhere, there is a very definite connection. It's something you're going to have to look for.
That was our Friday Night Connection tonight, put together by Paul Hardy of No. 8 Shaft Oak Close, Delves Lane Concert in County Durham.
A discerning musical gentleman.
Now, you're going to have to be very observant to get the answer to the Friday Night Connection tonight.
If you think you know what it is, jot it down on a postcard, letter card, any form of card you like.
And send it to The Friday Night Connection, The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Friday Night Connection, Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Do not forget, please, to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well.
Because that's half the fun.
Here's the third from our Session by Cryer, Footsteps Through Time.
You know something, when that band first came on the scene, I thought, guess what?
Changed my mind.
